description: All men are mortal. Socrates is a man. The rest is logic.

guides.text: A classic syllogism, attributed to Plato's most famous student, Aristotle. Every $x$ who is a man is mortal. Socrates is a man. Therefore: Socrates is mortal. This is the original example of logical deduction — two and a half millennia old.
Fun fact: Aristotle was one of Plato's most famous students.
Start by assuming the first [premise|premise]: $\forall x.\; man(x) \to mortal(x)$.
Now assume the second [premise|premise]: Socrates is a man.

The rule `forall-elim` instantiates a [universal-quantifier|universal quantifier]. From $\forall x.\; man(x) \to mortal(x)$ at step 1, replace $x$ with $socrates$ to get $man(socrates) \to mortal(socrates)$.
Now [modus-ponens|modus ponens]: $man(socrates) \to mortal(socrates)$ (step 3) and $man(socrates)$ (step 2) yield $mortal(socrates)$. The syllogism is complete.

unlocks.name: ∀-elim
unlocks.description: From $\forall x.\; \varphi$, derive $\varphi$ with any term $t$ substituted for $x$. What holds for everything holds for any particular thing.
